---
title: "Bayesian Inference with PseudoMarginalSolver"
author: "Simon Frost"
date: today
format:
  html:
    code-fold: false
    toc: true
  pdf:
    toc: true
  gfm:
    toc: true
engine: julia
julia:
  exeflags: ["--project=.."]
---

## Overview

The `PseudoMarginalSolver` combines **probabilistic ODE solving** with **Bayesian MCMC inference**. It forms an *unbiased* Monte-Carlo estimate of the marginal likelihood — drawing complete state trajectories from the probabilistic solver's Gauss–Markov posterior by forward-filter/backward-sampling (FFBS) and averaging the data likelihood over them — and uses it inside a random-walk Metropolis sampler. Because the estimator is unbiased and nonnegative, the chain targets the exact posterior (Andrieu & Roberts 2009).

This differs from:

- **MCMCSolver**: Uses deterministic ODE integration for the likelihood
- **MagiSolver**: Uses Kalman-filtered Integrated Brownian Motion to marginalize states
- **PseudoMarginalSolver**: Uses an unbiased Monte-Carlo (FFBS) estimate of the marginal likelihood inside random-walk Metropolis

```{julia}
using PartiallySpecifiedModels
using OrdinaryDiffEq
using MCMCChains
using Statistics
using Plots; default(fmt=:png)
using Random
Random.seed!(42)
```

## Logistic Growth Model

```{julia}
r_true(N) = 0.5 * (1.0 - N / 10.0)

function logistic!(du, u, p, t)
    N = u[1]
    du[1] = p.r(N) * N
end

sol_true = solve(ODEProblem(logistic!, [1.0], (0.0, 15.0), (; r=r_true)),
                 Tsit5(); saveat=1.0)
t_data = collect(sol_true.t)
data_N = [sol_true.u[i][1] + 0.1 * randn() for i in 1:length(t_data)]
data_matrix = reshape(max.(data_N, 0.01), :, 1)
```

### Observed Data

```{julia}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-data
#| fig-cap: "Simulated logistic growth data"
plot(t_data, data_N, seriestype=:scatter, ms=5, alpha=0.7,
    label="Observed", color=:steelblue, xlabel="Time", ylabel="Population N")
plot!(sol_true.t, [sol_true.u[i][1] for i in 1:length(sol_true.t)],
    label="True", color=:black, lw=2, ls=:dash)
title!("Logistic Growth Data")
```

### Fit with PseudoMarginalSolver

The Monte-Carlo likelihood estimate is noisy, so a good starting point helps
the random-walk sampler mix; we first run LAML to get a point estimate and
use it to warm-start the sampler.

```{julia}
uf = BSplineApproximator(:r, (0.0, 12.0), 6)

prob = PSMProblem(logistic!, [1.0], (0.0, 15.0), [uf];
    data_times=t_data, data_values=Float64.(data_matrix),
    obs_to_state=[1], known_params=NamedTuple(),
    likelihood=PartiallySpecifiedModels.Gaussian())

# LAML first for initialization
sol_laml = solve(prob, LAML(maxiters=50, verbose=false));
laml_init = Float64.(collect(sol_laml.parameters))

sol_pm = solve(prob, PseudoMarginalSolver(
    n_samples=200, n_warmup=100,
    n_steps=100, n_deriv=3,
    initial_params=laml_init,
    verbose=false));
```

### Compare with MCMCSolver

```{julia}
sol_mcmc = solve(prob, MCMCSolver(
    n_samples=200, n_warmup=100, verbose=false));
```

### Posterior Function with Credible Intervals

```{julia}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-posterior-function
#| fig-cap: "Posterior r(N) with 95% credible intervals from both Bayesian solvers"
chain_pm = sol_pm.convergence
chain_mcmc = sol_mcmc.convergence

N_grid = range(0.5, 10.0, length=150)
param_names_pm = names(chain_pm, :parameters)
param_names_mcmc = names(chain_mcmc, :parameters)

# Knot x-positions for the BSpline (6 knots over domain [0, 12])
knots_x = collect(range(0.0, 12.0, length=6))

# Helper: evaluate r(N) across N_grid for each posterior sample
function eval_posterior_curves(chain, pnames, knots_x, N_grid)
    # Get only the r[...] parameter columns
    r_names = filter(n -> startswith(string(n), "r["), pnames)
    samples = hcat([vec(Array(chain[:, n, :])) for n in r_names]...)
    curves = zeros(size(samples, 1), length(N_grid))
    for i in 1:size(samples, 1)
        evaluator = PartiallySpecifiedModels.build_bspline_evaluator(knots_x, samples[i, :])
        for (j, N) in enumerate(N_grid)
            curves[i, j] = evaluator(N)
        end
    end
    return curves
end

r_pm_curves = eval_posterior_curves(chain_pm, param_names_pm, knots_x, N_grid)
r_mcmc_curves = eval_posterior_curves(chain_mcmc, param_names_mcmc, knots_x, N_grid)

# Compute quantiles
pm_med = [quantile(r_pm_curves[:, j], 0.5) for j in 1:length(N_grid)]
pm_lo = [quantile(r_pm_curves[:, j], 0.025) for j in 1:length(N_grid)]
pm_hi = [quantile(r_pm_curves[:, j], 0.975) for j in 1:length(N_grid)]

mcmc_med = [quantile(r_mcmc_curves[:, j], 0.5) for j in 1:length(N_grid)]
mcmc_lo = [quantile(r_mcmc_curves[:, j], 0.025) for j in 1:length(N_grid)]
mcmc_hi = [quantile(r_mcmc_curves[:, j], 0.975) for j in 1:length(N_grid)]

p = plot(N_grid, r_true.(N_grid), lw=3, label="True r(N)", color=:black, ls=:dash,
    xlabel="Population N", ylabel="r(N)", title="Posterior Unknown Function")
plot!(p, N_grid, pm_med, ribbon=(pm_med .- pm_lo, pm_hi .- pm_med),
    fillalpha=0.2, lw=2, label="Pseudo-Marginal", color=:teal)
plot!(p, N_grid, mcmc_med, ribbon=(mcmc_med .- mcmc_lo, mcmc_hi .- mcmc_med),
    fillalpha=0.2, lw=2, label="MCMC", color=:coral)
p
```

### Trace Plots

```{julia}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-traces
#| fig-cap: "Trace plots for first three spline coefficients"
n_show = min(3, length(param_names_pm))
plts = []
for i in 1:n_show
    p = plot(vec(Array(chain_pm[:, param_names_pm[i], :])),
        label="Pseudo-Marginal", color=:teal, alpha=0.7,
        xlabel="Iteration", ylabel=string(param_names_pm[i]))
    plot!(p, vec(Array(chain_mcmc[:, param_names_mcmc[i], :])),
        label="MCMC", color=:coral, alpha=0.7)
    push!(plts, p)
end
plot(plts..., layout=(n_show, 1), size=(700, 200 * n_show))
```

### Fitted Trajectories

```{julia}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-trajectories
#| fig-cap: "Fitted population trajectories"
plot(t_data, data_N, seriestype=:scatter, ms=5, alpha=0.5,
    label="Data", color=:gray60, xlabel="Time", ylabel="Population N",
    title="Fitted Trajectories")
plot!(t_data, sol_pm.fitted_values[:, 1], lw=2, label="Pseudo-Marginal", color=:teal)
plot!(t_data, sol_mcmc.fitted_values[:, 1], lw=2, label="MCMC", color=:coral)
plot!(sol_true.t, [sol_true.u[i][1] for i in 1:length(sol_true.t)],
    lw=2, ls=:dash, label="True", color=:black)
```

## Bayesian Solver Comparison

| Feature | MCMCSolver | MagiSolver | PseudoMarginalSolver |
|---------|------------|------------|---------------------|
| **Inner likelihood** | Deterministic ODE | GP manifold constraint | Unbiased MC estimate (FFBS) |
| **Outer sampler** | NUTS | NUTS | Random-walk Metropolis |
| **State marginalization** | No (states from ODE) | No (states sampled) | Yes (MC-integrated over solver posterior) |
| **Unobserved states** | Requires all observed | Handles naturally | Handles via IBM |
| **ODE error** | Ignored | Modeled as noise | Modeled via IBM prior |
| **Output** | MCMCChains | MCMCChains | MCMCChains |

## Diagnostic Plots

A standard 4-panel diagnostic display assesses residual behaviour. The QQ plot checks normality of standardized residuals, "Residuals vs Fitted" detects systematic patterns, the histogram visualises the residual distribution, and "Observed vs Fitted" checks overall calibration.

```{julia}
using PartiallySpecifiedModels: appraise

diag = appraise(sol_pm)

p_qq = scatter(diag.qq_theoretical, diag.qq_sample,
    xlabel="Theoretical quantiles", ylabel="Sample quantiles",
    title="QQ Plot of Residuals", ms=3, legend=false, color=:steelblue)
mn, mx = extrema(vcat(diag.qq_theoretical, diag.qq_sample))
plot!(p_qq, [mn, mx], [mn, mx], color=:red, ls=:dash, label="")

p_rf = scatter(diag.fitted, diag.residuals,
    xlabel="Fitted values", ylabel="Residuals",
    title="Residuals vs Fitted", ms=3, legend=false, color=:steelblue)
hline!(p_rf, [0], color=:gray, ls=:dot)

p_hist = histogram(diag.residuals, normalize=:pdf,
    xlabel="Residuals", ylabel="Density",
    title="Histogram of Residuals", legend=false, color=:steelblue, alpha=0.7)

p_of = scatter(diag.observed, diag.fitted,
    xlabel="Observed", ylabel="Fitted",
    title="Observed vs Fitted", ms=3, legend=false, color=:steelblue)
mn2, mx2 = extrema(vcat(diag.observed, diag.fitted))
plot!(p_of, [mn2, mx2], [mn2, mx2], color=:red, ls=:dash, label="")

plot(p_qq, p_rf, p_hist, p_of, layout=(2, 2), size=(700, 600))
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
println("Durbin-Watson: ", join(["$(round(d, digits=3))" for d in diag.durbin_watson], ", "))
```

## Summary

The `PseudoMarginalSolver` provides full Bayesian inference over unknown function parameters using a probabilistic ODE solver for likelihood estimation. This naturally accounts for ODE discretization uncertainty, making it particularly appropriate when the ODE solution quality is uncertain or when the system is stiff.
